chunk_summary: Guidance on oral iron dosing for uncomplicated iron deficiency anemia, including counseling on administration, monitoring timelines, and response expectations.
learning_objectives: Understand the recommended dosing and administration of oral iron for uncomplicated iron deficiency anemia.
Learn counseling strategies to improve adherence and absorption of oral iron therapy.
Identify expected response timelines and monitoring protocols for therapeutic efficacy.
Recognize steps to take in cases of nonresponse or slower-than-expected improvement.
key_facts: Therapeutic oral elemental iron is typically prescribed at 3 mg/kg/day, with a range of 2–3 mg/kg/day for outpatient care.
Iron should be administered away from milk and calcium, ideally with vitamin C to enhance absorption.
Expected response includes reticulocytosis within 5–7 days and a hemoglobin rise of ~1 g/dL over 2–3 weeks.
Therapeutic dosing should continue until hemoglobin normalizes, followed by 2–3 months to replete iron stores.
Common side effects include dark stools and mild gastrointestinal upset; adherence can be improved by starting with a lower dose if nausea occurs.
Nonresponse should prompt re-checking of dose, adherence, and reassessment of underlying causes like malabsorption or inflammation.
reference_quote: Daily iron supplementation in children with iron deficiency anemia should be calculated based on elemental iron requirements (IAP standard treatment guidelines).
document_key_topics: Oral iron dosing and administration counseling
Monitoring response timelines in iron deficiency anemia
Adherence strategies and side effect management
Steps for addressing nonresponse to therapy
